elements.sent-2411-001: The traveling salesman problem is a widely studied classical combinatorial problem for which there are several integer linear formulations.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this work, we consider the Miller-Tucker-Zemlin (MTZ), Desrochers-Laporte (DL) and Single Commodity Flow (SCF) formulations.
elements.sent-2411-003: We argue that the choice of some parameters of these formulations is arbitrary and, therefore, there are families of formulations of which each of MTZ, DL, and SCF is a particular case.
elements.sent-2411-004: We analyze these families for different choices of the parameters, noting that in general the formulations involved are not comparable to each other and there is no one that dominates the rest.
elements.sent-2411-005: Then we define and study the closure of each family, that is, the set obtained by considering all the associated formulations simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-006: In particular, we give an explicit integer linear formulation for the closure of each of the families we have defined and then show how they compare to each other.
elements.sent-2411-007: Let $G=(N,A)$ be a complete directed graph on $n\geq4$ nodes, where $N=\{1,\ldots,n\}$ and $A=\{ij\tq i,j\in N,\ i\neq j\}$.
elements.sent-2411-008: A Hamiltonian cycle or {\em tour is a directed cycle in $G$ that begins and ends at the same node and such that each node is visited exactly once.}
elements.sent-2411-009: The Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem (ATSP) seeks to find a Hamiltonian cycle of minimum cost with respect to a given vector $c\in\RR^A$.
elements.sent-2411-010: It finds a number or applications in logistics, sequencing, scheduling, among others.
elements.sent-2411-011: We refer the reader to \cite{applegate2006traveling for a thorough coverage of history, applications, and solution approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-012: A generic mixed-integer linear programming formulation for the ATSP using binary variables $x_{ij}$ for $ij\in A$ can be written as \begin{equation}\min \quad \sum_{ij\in A}c_{ij}x_{ij}\label{eq:obj_fun} s.t.\quad \sum_{ij\in\delta^+(i)} x_{ij} = 1\quad for all\ i\in N\label{AssignPolytope1} \sum_{ji\in\delta^-(i)} x_{ji} = 1\quad for all\ i\in N\label{AssignPolytope2} \{ij\in A\tq x_{ij}=1\}\ does not contain subtours\label{nosubtours} x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}\quad for all\ ij\in A \label{AssignPolytope3},\end{equation} where $\delta^+(i)$ is the set arcs originating from node $i$, $\delta^-(i)$ is the set of arcs arriving to node $i$ (see below for a formal definition of these sets of arcs) and a {\em subtour is a cycle in $G$ that does not cover all nodes in $N$.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\min \quad \sum_{ij\in A}c_{ij}x_{ij}\label{eq:obj_fun} s.t.\quad \sum_{ij\in\delta^+(i)} x_{ij} = 1\quad for all\ i\in N\label{AssignPolytope1} \sum_{ji\in\delta^-(i)} x_{ji} = 1\quad for all\ i\in N\label{AssignPolytope2} \{ij\in A\tq x_{ij}=1\}\ does not contain subtours\label{nosubtours} x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}\quad for all\ ij\in A \label{AssignPolytope3},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-013: In the optimization problem above, the objective function to be minimized is given by~\eqref{eq:obj_fun , constraints \eqref{AssignPolytope1 , \eqref{AssignPolytope2 and \eqref{AssignPolytope3 define an integer linear programming formulation for a restricted version of the {\em Assignment Problem (equivalent to the face of the assignment polytope given by the equations $x_{ii =0$ for all $i\in N$), while requirement \eqref{nosubtours ensures that any feasible solution represents a single tour.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Requirement \eqref{nosubtours can be written using linear constraints on the $x$ variables (a formulation in the original space) or using additional variables and linear constraints (an extended formulation).}
elements.sent-2411-015: In this paper, we study properties of {\em parametric integer programming formulations for the ATSP that are based on three classic formulations from the literature: the Miller-Tucker-Zemlin (MTZ), the Desrochers–Laporte (DL), and the Single-Commodity Flow (SCF) formulations.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Before stating our results, we give some notation, precisely describe the MTZ, DL, and SCF formulations, and introduce the parametric formulations proposed in this paper: the $d$-MTZ, $d$-DL and $b$-SCF formulations.
elements.sent-2411-017: For $S\subseteq N$, let $\delta^+(S)=\{ij\in A\tq i\in S,\ j\in N\setminus S\}$, $\delta^-(S)=\{ij\in A\tq i\in N\setminus S,\ j\in S\}$, and $A(S)=\{ij\in A\tq i\in S,\ j\in S\}$.
elements.sent-2411-019: Denote $N_1=N\setminus\{1\}=\{2,\ldots,n\}$ and $A_1=\{ij\tq i,j\in N_1,\ i\neq j\}$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Let $\mathcal C_1$ be the set of directed cycles with arcs in $A_1$ and let $\mathcal S_1$ be the set of subsets of $N_1$ of size at least 2.
elements.sent-2411-021: For a cycle $C$, its reversed cycle will be denoted $\revcycle{C =\{ji\tq ij\in C\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Given a set $Q=\{(x,u)\in\RR^{n_1}\times\RR^{n_2}\tq Ax+Bu\leq b\}$, its projection onto the $x$ variables is denoted as $\proj_x(Q)=\{x\in\RR^{n_1}\tq \exists u\in\RR^{n_2}\ \st\ (x,u)\in Q\}$.
elements.sent-2411-023: The vector with all its components equal to one will be denoted $\ones$; its dimension must be understood by the context in which the notation is used.
elements.sent-2411-024: In addition, given a set of indices $K$, $\ones_K$ denotes a binary vector whose nonzero components are the ones indexed by $K$.
elements.sent-2411-025: Finally, $\RR_+$ and $\RR_{++}$ stand for the nonnegative and positive real numbers, respectively.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Classic integer programming formulations for the ATSP
elements.sent-2411-026: Below we present some of the most commonly used integer programming formulations for the ATSP.
elements.sent-2411-027: We refer the reader to \cite{oncan2009comparative for a thorough survey on different formulations for the ATSP and relations among them.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In all formulations that follow, for modeling purposes and without loss of generality, we consider node 1 as a special node.
elements.sent-2411-029: The Dantzig-Fulkerson-Johnson (DFJ) formulation \cite{dantzig1954solution writes \eqref{nosubtours in terms of the variables $x_{ij}$ for $ij\in A$ by using the exponentially many constraints known as {\em Clique inequalities : \begin{equation}\label{eq:DFJ_ineq} \sum_{ij\in A(S)}x_{ij}\leq |S|-1\quadfor all\ S\in\mathcal S_1.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The above inequalities can be replaced by the {\em Cut inequalities : \begin{equation}\label{eq:DFJ_cut} \sum_{ij\in\delta^+(S)}x_{ij}\geq 1\quadfor all\ S\in\mathcal S_1,\end{equation} obtaining an equivalent formulation.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The Miller-Tucker-Zemlin (MTZ) formulation \cite{miller1960integer , a compact extended formulation, uses additional continuous variables $u_2,\ldots,u_n\in\RR$ and the quadratically many inequalities \begin{equation}\label{eq:MTZ_ineq} u_i-u_j+(n-1)x_{ij}\leq n-2\quadfor all\ ij\in A_1.\end{equation}.}

elements.subsection-2411-002: New parametric integer programming formulations for the ATSP
elements.sent-2411-041: We define new formulations for the ATSP as follows: for each of the classic MTZ, DL, and SCF formulations, we keep the assignment polytope constraints unchanged and we replace some of the numbers appearing in the remaining constraints by appropriate parameters $d\in\RR^{A_1 _{++}$ or $b\in\RR_{++ ^{N_1}$, depending on the classic formulation being considered.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The $d$-MTZ formulation is defined by replacing constraints~\eqref{eq:MTZ_ineq in the MTZ formulation by \begin{equation}\label{eq:genMTZ_ineq} u_i-u_j+d_{ij}\leq 1-x_{ij}\quad ij\in A_1.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The $d$-DL formulation is given by the replacing the constraints~\eqref{eq:DL_ineq in the DL formulation by \begin{equation}\label{eq:genDL_ineq} u_i-u_j+x_{ij}+(1-d_{ij}-d_{ji})x_{ji}\leq 1-d_{ij}\quad for all\ ij\in A_1.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The $b$-SCF formulation is obtained by replacing the constraints~\eqref{eq:scf_balance -\eqref{eq:scf_bound in the SCF formulation by \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:genSCF_ineq} \sum_{ij\in\delta^+(i)}f_{ij}-\sum_{ji\in\delta^-(i)}f_{ji}=\begin{cases}1 i=1 -b_i i\in N_1\end{cases}\label{eq:genSCF1} f_{ij}\leq x_{ij}\quad for all\ ij\in A\label{eq:genSCF2}.\end{eqnarray}.}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\label{eq:genSCF_ineq} \sum_{ij\in\delta^+(i)}f_{ij}-\sum_{ji\in\delta^-(i)}f_{ji}=1 i=1 -b_i i\in N_1\label{eq:genSCF1} f_{ij}\leq x_{ij}\quad for all\ ij\in A\label{eq:genSCF2}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-045: We remark here that in order for these to be valid formulations for the ATSP, the parameters $d\in\RR^{A_1 _{++}$ and $b\in\RR_{++ ^{N_1}$ must be chosen appropriately.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: We define \begin{equation}D=\left\{d\in\RR^{A_1}_{++}\tq \sum_{ij\in C}d_{ij}\leq 1\ \forall C\in\mathcal C_1\right\}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}B=\left\{b\in\RR^{N_1}_{++}\tq \sum_{i\in N_1}b_i=1\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-047: In the sections corresponding to each of the proposed formulations, we argue that \eqref{eq:genMTZ_ineq or \eqref{eq:genDL_ineq with $d\in D$, and \eqref{eq:genSCF_ineq with $b\in B$ provide valid formulations for the ATSP.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Each formulation that we have presented has a polyhedron associated to it which is obtained by removing all integrality constraints from the corresponding formulation.
elements.sent-2411-049: We denote the polyhedron associated to the (restricted) Assignment Problem as $P_\ap=\{x\in[0,1]^A\tq x\ \text{satisfies \eqref{AssignPolytope1 and \eqref{AssignPolytope2 }\}$.}}

elements.subsection-2411-003: Our results and organization of the paper
elements.sent-2411-052: In this paper, we study the following properties of the formulations defined in the previous section:.
elements.sent-2411-053: We study the formulations $P_\mtz(d)$, $P_\dl(d)$, and $P_\scf(b)$ that are obtained by projecting the extended formulations $Q_\mtz(d)$, $Q_\dl(d)$, and $Q_\scf(b)$ onto the space of $x$-variables.
elements.sent-2411-054: In particular, we give a full polyhedral description and characterize their facets.
elements.sent-2411-055: Two formulations are comparable if one it is included in the other (the formulation that is included in the other is said to be stronger).
elements.sent-2411-056: We show that in general for $d,d'\in D$, $P_\mtz(d)$ and $P_\mtz(d')$ are not comparable; we also give conditions for which given $d$ we can find a parameter $d'$ that gives a stronger formulation.
elements.sent-2411-057: We also show that for $d,d'\in D$, under a minor condition, the formulations $P_\dl(d)$ and $P_\dl(d')$ are not comparable.
elements.sent-2411-058: Finally, we show that for $b,b'\in B$, the formulations $P_\scf(d)$ and $P_\scf(d')$ are never comparable.
elements.sent-2411-059: We define the closure of a family of parametric formulations as the set obtained by simultaneously considering the formulations for all values of the parameters.
elements.sent-2411-060: More precisely, for the $d$-$\mtz$ formulations the closure is the set $\bigcap_{d\in D P_\mtz(d)$, for the $d$-$\dl$ formulations the closure is the set $\bigcap_{d\in D P_\dl(d)$ and for the $b$-$\scf$ formulations the closure is the set $\bigcap_{b\in B P_\scf(d)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: We completely characterize all these closures and study some of their properties.
elements.sent-2411-062: The rest of the paper is organized as follows: in Section~\ref{sec:prelim we define general parametric formulations, the closure of a family of parametric formulations and related concepts, and study their properties.}

elements.subsection-2411-004: General parametric formulations and extended formulations
elements.sent-2411-066: Since in this paper we consider several formulations for the ASTP, it is convenient to study properties of formulations and extended formulations of sets of integral vectors in more generality.
elements.sent-2411-067: An extended formulation of $S$ is a set $Q\subseteq\RR^{n_1}\times E$, where $E$ is an arbitrary set, such that $S=\proj_{x (Q)\cap\ZZ^{n_1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The parametric (extended) formulations we study are polyhedra, and the parameters can appear in the constraint matrix or in the r.h.s..
elements.sent-2411-069: The following definition encompasses all the formulations we consider and gives a precise way to compare formulations for different parameters.
elements.sent-2411-070: Given $(A,G,b)\in \PP$, we consider the set $P(A,G,b)=\{x \in X\tq \exists u\in\RR^{n_2}\ s.t.\ A x+Gu\leq b\}$.
elements.sent-2411-071: We say that $P(A,G,b)$ with the set $\PP$ is a parametric formulation for $S$ if $P(A,G,b)\cap\ZZ^{n_1 = S$ for all $(A,G,b)\in \PP$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: For $(A,G,b),(A',G',b')\in \PP$ we say that $P(A,G,b)$ dominates $P(A',G',b')$ if $P(A,G,b)\subseteq P(A',G',b')$; we say that $P(A,G,b)$ and $P(A',G',b')$ are comparable if one of these formulations dominates the other.

elements.sent-2411-387: In this work, we have introduced parametric formulations for the ATSP based on the MZT, DL, and SCF formulations.
elements.sent-2411-388: We showed how these formulations arise and how they compare in terms of the choice of parameters and in terms of their closures.
elements.sent-2411-389: A natural question is how to apply these results computationally to solve instances of the ATSP.
elements.sent-2411-390: We envision a scheme where we start with the extended formulation given by a single element of a parametric family (a particular choice of the parameters), and then we enrich this formulation by including variables and constraints associated to more elements of the family.
elements.sent-2411-391: It would be interesting to study how to select the parameters in a dynamic fashion, as we might need criteria different from the notion of a most-violated constraint given a fractional solution.
elements.sent-2411-392: Another research direction is to apply the framework of parametric formulations to other combinatorial problems whose natural or classic formulations can be generalized.
